title: Light-Induced Even-Wave Spin Splittings in Nonmagnetic Centrosymmetric Systems with Spin-Orbit Coupling
authors: Xiao-Jiao Wang, Dongling Liu, Di Zhu, Zheng-Yang Zhuang, Zhongbo Yan
abstract: Spin splitting underpins a vast range of spin-dependent phenomena. Traditionally, two primary mechanisms generate such splitting: relativistic spin-orbit coupling (SOC) and nonrelativistic magnetic exchange coupling (MEC). Governed by distinct symmetry constraints, they produce splittings of opposite parity -- odd for SOC and even for MEC -- a dichotomy that underpins the distinct spin physics of nonmagnetic and magnetic systems. In this work, we break this dichotomy by demonstrating the dynamic generation of even-parity spin splitting in centrosymmetric, nonmagnetic systems driven by circularly polarized light. We show that the symmetry of the induced splitting is controlled by the angular character of the underlying orbitals, enabling the realization of s-wave, d-wave, and g-wave spin-split band structures identical to those of ferromagnets and altermagnets. Furthermore, we find that these spin-split bands can naturally host a Chern insulator phase. We also discuss the associated spin and orbital magnetization. Our results establish a direct and previously unrecognized conceptual link between the two fundamental mechanisms of spin splitting.
summary: This paper shows that circularly polarized light can induce even-parity spin splitting in nonmagnetic, centrosymmetric materials, effectively mimicking the spin physics of ferromagnets and altermagnets. By controlling the orbital character, one can realize various spin-split symmetries and even topological Chern insulator phases. This provides a new pathway for light-induced control of spin and topological properties without intrinsic magnetism.
main_problem: The paper investigates breaking the traditional dichotomy between spin-orbit coupling (odd-parity) and magnetic exchange coupling (even-parity) by generating even-parity spin splitting in nonmagnetic, centrosymmetric systems using light.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that circularly polarized light can dynamically generate even-parity spin splitting with s-, d-, and g-wave symmetries, and that these driven states can host Chern insulator phases.
method: The study employs Floquet-Bloch theory and a high-frequency, off-resonant expansion to derive an effective Hamiltonian for the time-periodic system.
model_or_system: The model consists of 2D, nonmagnetic, centrosymmetric systems with spin-orbit coupling, including square lattice models with p-orbitals (s-wave) and d-orbitals (d- and g-wave).
key_observables: Spin-splitting parity, spin-split energy spectra, Chern number, spin magnetization, and orbital magnetization.
important_parameters: Driving frequency (high-frequency regime), light amplitude, light helicity, spin-orbit coupling strength, and orbital angular character.
main_assumptions: The analysis is performed in the high-frequency, off-resonant regime where the driving frequency is much larger than the bandwidth.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the symmetry dichotomy and the new light-induced mechanism; Figure 2 shows energy bands and isoenergy contours for s-wave splitting; Figure E1 shows g-wave spin texture isoenergy contours.
paper_structure: The paper begins by establishing the symmetry dichotomy, introduces the Floquet-driven model, analyzes specific orbital-dependent splitting symmetries (s, d, and g-wave), discusses topological phases and magnetization, and concludes with material candidates and experimental signatures.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Conventional picture of spin splitting induced by SOC and MEC. Starting from a static Hamiltonian H0 that preserves spin- SU(2), T and P, the inclusion of T -invariant and P-broken SOC (HSO) leads to odd-parity spin splitting, whereas the inclusion of T - broken and P-invariant MEC (HM) leads to even-parity spin split- ting. (b) A counterpart to the conventional picture. Starting from a CPL-driven Hamiltonian HF that preserves spin-SU(2) and P but lacks T , the inclusion of T -invariant and P-invariant SOC leads to even-parity spin splitting, whereas the inclusion of T -broken and P- broken MEC (preserving the combination of a π spin rotation and a spatial inversion) leads to...

FIG. 2. Light-induced extended s-wave spin splitting. Energy bands along high-symmetry lines for the conduction-valence-band symmet- ric [(a), t1 = 0] and asymmetric [(c), t1 = 0.1] case, respectively. Orange and blue lines represent spin-up and spin-down bands, re- spectively. Panels (b) and (d) show the isoenergy contours at E = 1, with (b) [(d)] corresponding to (a) [(c)]. In (d), the dashed rings cor- responds to a (π, π) shift of the solid rings centering round the M point. Shared parameters are: t2 = 1, t3 = 0.5, λ = 0.5, ω = 5, η = 1, and A0 = 1.0.

FIG. 3. (a) Topological phase diagram. (b) Open-boundary spectrum for the Chern insulator phase labeled by the black dot illustrated in panel (a). Each edge contains two spin-up and two spin-down chiral edge states, consistent with the Chern number C = 4. Shared pa- rameters are: t1 = 0.1, t2 = 1, t3 = 0.5, ω = 5, and η = 1.

FIG. 4. Light-induced d-wave spin splitting. (a) Energy bands along high-symmetry lines. Orange and blue curves denote spin-up and spin-down bands, respectively; grey curves indicate spin-degenerate bands. (b) Isoenergy contours at E = 0.8. The alternating orange and blue lobes reveal a dxy-wave symmetry. Such a spin texture is a hallmark of d-wave altermagnetism, here induced without intrinsic magnetism. Parameters are: t+ = 0.5, t−= 1, t3 = 0.5, λ = 0.5, ω = 5, η = 1, and A0 = 1.

FIG. 5. Evolution of spin and orbital magnetizations as functions of the driving amplitude. The electron filling fraction is fixed at 1. (a) Evolution in the s-wave spin-split phase. (b) Evolution in the d-wave spin-split phase. The unit of M (s) z is µB, and the unit of M (o) z is µ∗ B = ℏe/2m∗, where m∗is the effective mass defined at the band edge of the lower band before driving. Parameters for (a): {t1, t2, t3, λ, ω, η, A0} = {0.5, 1, 0.5, 0.5, 5, 1, 1}. Parameters for (b): {t+, t−, t3, λ, ω, η, A0} = {0.5, 1, 0.5, 0.5, 5, 1, 1}.
